Title: 5 Semiconductor Stocks To Buy And 1 To Ignore
URL: https://www.forbes.com/sites/investor-hub/article/semiconductor-stocks-to-buy-and-ignore/
Time Published: 2026-07-21T20:53:39Z
Full Content:
ByJason Kirsch, Contributor. Edited bySophia Acevedo, Associate Editor. The semiconductor sector has performed well this year overall. AI infrastructure spending has been a significant tailwind for growth this year. However, not every chip stock deserves a spot in your portfolio, so it's important to favor companies with strong fundamentals and durable AI-driven growth. Top recommendations include NVIDIA, the undisputed AI chip leader; TSMC, the global foundry backbone; Micron, capitalizing on insatiable memory demand; Broadcom, dominant in custom AI accelerators; and Lam Research, a key supplier of wafer fabrication equipment. Despite recent stock surges, Intel may not be the as compelling of a choice. Investors should manage volatility through dollar-cost averaging and diversified portfolios across the AI supply chain, while closely monitoring valuations. The semiconductor sector is having a moment, even despite recent volatility. The benchmark Invesco PHLX Semiconductor ETF (SOXQ) is up roughly 100% year to date in 2026, easily lapping the broader tech sector. Behind the surge is a relentless wave of AI infrastructure spending from hyperscalers like Microsoft, Google and Meta, which are collectively expected to deploy nearly $700 billion in capital expenditures in 2026, with the majority earmarked for AI data centers. That's a massive tailwind for chipmakers across the supply chain. But not every chip stock deserves a spot in your portfolio. With valuations stretched across much of the sector, selectivity matters more than ever. I’ve identified five semiconductor stocks with compelling fundamentals and durable AI-driven growth catalysts — and one that investors may want to sidestep despite the hype surrounding it. Picking semiconductor stocks requires more than riding momentum. For the buy list, the focus was on companies with structural advantages: dominant market positions, improving earnings trajectories and direct exposure to the parts of the AI supply chain where demand is structurally supply-constrained rather than cyclically elevated. Revenue acceleration, analyst conviction and forward earnings estimates were all weighted heavily. AI spending is expected to stay strong in 2026, justifying premium valuations for the highest-quality companies. The stock to ignore was identified by the inverse criteria: where enthusiasm has outrun execution, where near-term profitability remains elusive despite a surging share price, and where the competitive position is more contested than the market currently prices in. Elevated valuations remain a common theme across much of the semiconductor industry, and distinguishing between AI beneficiaries and AI hype is the core task here. NVIDIA remains the undisputed engine of the AI chip revolution. In fiscal year 2026, NVIDIA’s revenue reached $215.9 billion — a 65% increase versus the prior year — with earnings growing at nearly the same pace. The company’s GPU architecture has become the default infrastructure for training and running AI models, cementing a competitive moat that rivals have struggled to breach. Despite the monster run, 48 analysts maintain a "Strong Buy" rating with a consensus price target implying roughly 46% upside from current levels. Nvidia estimates it can sell a trillion dollars' worth of data center AI chips in 2026 and 2027 combined — a number that, remarkably, looks achievable given current demand signals. The company has also identified a $200 billion AI CPU inference opportunity, expanding its total addressable market well beyond GPUs. With wafer-fabrication equipment spending accelerating and hyperscalers placing multi-year orders, Nvidia's revenue visibility is as strong as any company in tech. For investors with a multi-year horizon, NVDA remains the single clearest expression of the AI hardware megatrend. TSMC controls 72% of the global chip foundry market, making it the physical backbone of the AI supply chain. Every major AI chip — from NVIDIA’s GPUs to Broadcom’s custom ASICs — runs through TSMC’s fabs. Its dominance in advanced nodes including 3nm volume production and 2nm ramp-up gives it a near-irreplaceable strategic position. In 2026, TSMC has also raised its full-year revenue growth guidance. TSMC’s competitive moat is structural, not cyclical. Building a world-class semiconductor foundry takes years and tens of billions in capital investment — a barrier that makes TSMC’s position self-reinforcing. The adoption of agentic AI is expected to drive another wave of demand, as AI agents processing tasks without human interaction require significantly more token generation and compute capacity. Investors should monitor geopolitical risk given TSMC's Taiwan base, but for long-term holders, the company's combination of pricing power, technology leadership and AI-driven growth makes it one of the sector's most compelling holds. Micron is the memory play on AI infrastructure, and its numbers have turned extraordinary. Revenue spiked 196%, and non-GAAP earnings surged 682% year over year in its fiscal Q2 2026. The reason is structural: AI context lengths are increasing 30x per year while memory content per server has doubled in three years, creating an insatiable demand for DRAM and high-bandwidth memory (HBM) that Micron is uniquely positioned to supply. The company has already sold out its HBM supply for 2026, with significant 2027 capacity committed under long-term agreements. Morgan Stanley analysts had named Micron their top semiconductor pick for early 2026, arguing the ongoing AI buildout has created the most severe DRAM and NAND shortage in three decades. That shortage is structurally driving prices higher. With most major analysts in "buy" mode and memory demand showing no sign of softening, MU offers a compelling combination of growth and value within the chip sector. Broadcom has emerged as the dominant supplier of custom AI accelerators (ASICs), holding 70–80% market share in a segment that counts Alphabet’s Google, Meta, ByteDance, OpenAI and Anthropic among its customers. Unlike NVIDIA’s general-purpose GPUs, Broadcom's ASICs are purpose-built for each hyperscaler's specific AI workloads, offering performance and efficiency advantages at scale. Q2 revenues rose 47.9% year over year, with AI chip revenue alone reaching $10.8 billion in the quarter. The AI accelerator market is projected to expand at 29% annually through 2033, and Broadcom’s customer pipeline continues to grow. Beyond chips, Broadcom’s VMware acquisition adds a recurring software revenue stream in data center virtualization. That dual engine of custom AI chips and enterprise software makes AVGO one of the more diversified and durable names in the semiconductor space. Lam Research makes the machines that make the chips — specifically, deposition and etch equipment used by foundries and memory manufacturers. It’s a pick-and-shovel play on the entire AI semiconductor build-out. Lam raised its 2026 wafer fabrication equipment (WFE) spending forecast to $140 billion, and third-party estimates from Counterpoint Research put the number even higher at $159 billion. Whether chips are made for AI training, inference or memory, Lam's equipment is almost always in the manufacturing chain. The beauty of Lam’s position is that it benefits regardless of which AI chip design wins. Consensus estimates project a 26% revenue increase in FY2026 followed by a 31% jump in FY2027, and Bank of America projects the global semiconductor market could clock a 28% CAGR through 2030, topping $2 trillion in annual revenue. That sustained growth cycle translates directly into sustained WFE demand. For investors who want AI chip exposure with less concentration risk in any single chip designer, LRCX offers an elegant upstream alternative. Intel’s stock has surged on AI optimism, powered by government investment, new foundry contracts and a narrative of turnaround. But the underlying financials tell a more complicated story. Intel's chip manufacturing segment posted a $2.4 billion operating loss in Q1 2026, despite $5.4 billion in sales. The foundry business remains deeply unprofitable, and while deals with Apple, Microsoft and SpaceX are promising, they have not yet yielded meaningful financial gains. Morningstar does not believe Intel has an economic moat. Morningstar has argued that its manufacturing setbacks have eliminated competitive advantages that chip design expertise alone cannot restore. Intel’s returns on invested capital have fallen in recent years, and Morningstar foresees no excess returns on capital in the years ahead. Until the foundry business turns profitable and Intel demonstrates sustained execution, the risk/reward doesn't justify the current valuation. Even the best semiconductor stocks can be volatile, and 2026 has offered a clear reminder of that. For example, in early June, AMD and Intel shares dropped more than 10% in a single session following cautious AI chip guidance from Broadcom — despite the broader AI thesis remaining intact. For investors, the practical lesson is that single-session drawdowns in high-growth sectors are common. Dollar-cost averaging — investing a fixed amount at regular intervals regardless of price — is one of the most effective tools for managing this kind of volatility without trying to time the market. Portfolio construction matters too. The AI semiconductor opportunity extends well beyond a handful of mega-cap names, with critical roles played across memory, connectivity, optical interconnects, power efficiency and compute infrastructure. Spreading exposure across the supply chain — GPU designers, foundries, memory makers and equipment suppliers — reduces concentration risk in any single company or earnings cycle. A position in LRCX, for example, is a very different bet than a position in NVDA, even though both benefit from the same underlying trend. Finally, keep an eye on valuation anchors. Semiconductor stocks are inherently cyclical, and even structurally strong companies can trade well ahead of their fundamentals during a momentum-driven rally. Checking forward P/E ratios, revenue growth rates and analyst price targets regularly — and being willing to trim positions that have run far ahead of fair value — is a disciplined approach that protects long-term gains. Volatility in a secular growth sector is most dangerous when it catches investors with undiversified, over-leveraged positions. The AI chip cycle is real, the demand is structural, and some stocks offer distinct, high-quality entry points across the semiconductor supply chain. Intel, despite its dramatic share price recovery, remains a turnaround story with execution risk and a foundry business that's still burning cash. In a sector this hot, knowing what to avoid is just as important as knowing what to buy. What Makes a Semiconductor Stock A Strong "Buy"? A strong semiconductor buy combines durable competitive positioning with clear, near-term earnings acceleration. Specifically, the best picks tend to dominate a scarce or supply-constrained layer of the AI chip supply chain, have improving analyst consensus, and trade at valuations supported by credible multi-year growth projections. Companies with direct exposure to AI infrastructure spending — where hyperscaler capex has already been committed — offer the most visibility into sustained demand. Why Should Certain Tech Or Chip Stocks Be Ignored Despite High Market Interest? High market interest alone is not a fundamental reason to buy a stock — and in semiconductors, it can be a warning sign. Elevated valuations remain a common theme across much of the semiconductor industry after the sector's AI-driven rally, and stocks that have surged on narrative rather than earnings tend to punish latecomers most severely. The key red flags to watch for: unprofitable core segments, no sustainable competitive moat, and a share price that already prices in a flawless execution scenario that the company has yet to demonstrate. How Does Cyclicality Impact High-Growth AI Semiconductor Stocks? Cyclicality is baked into the semiconductor industry's DNA, and the AI boom doesn't eliminate it — it just shifts the cycle's timing. Even during the current bull run, inventory corrections, memory supply imbalances, and demand shifts between consumer electronics and data centers can create sharp swings in revenue and stock prices. The best strategy is to focus on companies with structural AI tailwinds rather than cyclically elevated demand, and to size positions with enough patience to ride out the inevitable downturns that come with owning high-growth chip stocks.

Title: Collect 10% On META Stock Now, Own It 30% Cheaper If It Dips
URL: https://www.trefis.com/articles/608159/collect-10-on-meta-stock-now-own-it-30-cheaper-if-it-dips/2026-07-21
Time Published: 2026-07-21T17:51:23Z
Full Content:
Collect upfront option premium while agreeing to potentially buy the stock at a lower target price if assigned. Meta Platforms (META) has been a paradox for investors, with shares trading around $645.85, well off their highs and trailing the broader market over the past year. Yet beneath the stock chart is a business reporting formidable growth. This creates an interesting setup for investors who see long-term value but are wary of the current price: a way to get paid a healthy income now for simply agreeing to buy the shares at a significant discount, should they ever get that cheap. If META stays above $450 through 6/17/2027, the put expires worthless and you simply keep the full $2,228 premium. That is about 5.0% on the $45,000 you set aside over 332 days, cash that might otherwise earn you 5.0% or so. You never buy the stock and keep the income, free to do it again. If META closes below $450, you are assigned and buy 100 shares at $450. The $2,228 premium you already pocketed lowers your effective cost to about $427.73 a share, roughly a 34% discount to today’s price, though if the stock has fallen further by then you would be holding a paper loss. So what happens if META really does close below $450, and you are the one buying? Then everything rests on a single question. So, what kind of company would you be getting into if the stock dropped and you were asked to buy? On one hand, you’re buying into an advertising juggernaut that is performing at the top of its game. The most recent quarter saw total revenue jump 33%, fueled by a 19% increase in ad impressions and a 12% rise in the average price per ad. This isn’t just a rising tide; the company’s AI-powered ad tools are delivering real results. This is the cash-flow machine that funds everything else. But that everything else is the source of the market’s anxiety and the real risk in this trade. Meta is in the middle of one of the most aggressive capital expenditure cycles in corporate history, with a 2026 forecast of “$125 billion to $145 billion”. The company recently disclosed a “$107 billion step-up in our contractual commitments” for infrastructure, and management openly admits they have “continued to underestimate our compute needs.” This spending is for a future of AI agents and personal superintelligence where the path to profit is still being sketched out. Management acknowledges they don’t have a “very precise plan for exactly how each product is going to scale,” and monetization ideas are still in the “imagine commission structures or a premium offering” phase. This is the bargain you’re being offered. You get paid to underwrite that uncertainty. If the market’s skepticism about this massive AI spend deepens and pushes the stock lower, you become an owner. If the market rewards the company for its core business strength and long-term vision, you simply keep the income you were paid upfront. The question of whether this is a steal or a trap is a live one, and it’s worth considering both sides of the argument. Ultimately, the decision comes down to whether you believe the current advertising engine is strong enough to carry the company through this colossal investment phase. The trade pays you to wait for the answer. The one thing to watch, then, is the return on that investment. Keep an eye on capital expenditures and any commentary that connects that spending to new, tangible products with clear monetization paths. If that connection becomes clearer, the market’s fears may ease. If the spending continues to escalate without a clear payoff in sight, the stock could easily find its way down to your price. Wondering whether another stock offers a better yield, or what this same trade would pay on a name you already like? You can screen the latest cash-secured put yields across the market for yourself. And if it is exposure to communication services as a whole you want rather than this one name, a communication services ETF like XLC covers that single sector. Going broader than any one sector, to a quality-first mix across the whole market, is where the portfolio below comes in. A put sale is a promise to add to a single name, and the first thing a professional checks before that promise is existing exposure, because concentration is what turns an income trade into an oversized bet. That check is exactly what the Trefis Wealth team provides, with the same rules-based systematic discipline that runs our High Quality Portfolio. Title: Prediction: Up 144% YTD, Is Arm Holdings The Next Nvidia?
URL: https://247wallst.com/investing/2026/07/21/prediction-up-144-ytd-is-arm-holdings-the-next-nvidia/
Time Published: 2026-07-21T17:30:53Z
Full Content:
Investing ARM surged 144% YTD, with data center royalties more than doubling and $2 billion in contracted AGI CPU demand backing a BUY target of $302. ARM collects royalties on NVDA's Vera CPU and major hyperscaler designs, replicating the custom silicon trajectory that drove AVGO to 143% AI revenue growth. A trailing P/E of 311 and a pending Qualcomm trial are the biggest threats to the thesis, with the bear scenario pricing ARM at $238. Act now: the analyst who called NVIDIA in 2010 just named his top 10 AI stocks — and Arm didn't make the cut. Grab the names FREE today. Sending You to Google News in 3 © TechAnimationStock / Shutterstock.com Arm’s run in 2026 has been one of the sharpest re-ratings in large-cap tech. Shares of Arm Holdings (NASDAQ:ARM | ARM Price Prediction) trade at $271.49 as of July 20, 2026, up 144.43% year to date on the back of a data center royalty explosion and the launch of Arm’s first production silicon. Our 24/7 Wall St. price target for Arm is $301.87, implying 11.19% upside over the next twelve months. The action is buy, with a confidence level of 90%. Arm bottomed near $105.78 in January before ripping to a June high of $396.34 and pulling back to today’s level. The stock is off 17.38% over the past week and 36.21% over the past month, sitting 33% below its 52-week high of $452.70. In Q4 FY2026, Arm posted revenue of $1.49 billion, up 20.06% year over year, with non-GAAP EPS of $0.60 beating the $0.5793 consensus. License revenue jumped 29% and data center royalty revenue more than doubled year over year. Full-year FY2026 revenue reached $4.92 billion, up 22.79%, a third straight year above 20% growth. The bull case rests on more than $2 billion in customer demand for the Arm AGI CPU across FY27 and FY28. Meta is the lead partner on a multi-generation roadmap targeting 3+ billion users. Google is replacing x86 host processors with custom Arm-based Axion CPUs in next-gen TPUs. NVIDIA announced Vera, its next Arm-based CPU. Microsoft is expanding Cobalt across Azure. Arm claims roughly 50% CPU compute share among top hyperscalers. Management is tracking toward a $15 billion silicon business forecast against a data center CPU market that could exceed $100 billion by 2030. If the AGI CPU ramp materializes, the bull-case scenario points to $434.24 within twelve months, a 59.95% return. Valuation is the biggest hurdle. Arm trades at a trailing P/E of 311 and a forward P/E of 122. Non-GAAP operating margin compressed from 52.8% to 49.1% as R&D spending jumped 43% to $1.911 billion. Bulls note this reflects deliberate investment in AGI CPU engineering that should scale as royalties ramp. The Qualcomm/Nuvia trial expected in Q4 calendar 2026, SoftBank’s controlling stake, and export-control risk all weigh. The bear scenario points to $238.31, a 12.22% drawdown. NVIDIA (NASDAQ:NVDA) trades at $202.81 with a YTD gain of just 8.88%, versus Arm’s 144%. Arm trails Nvidia in scale, yet its royalty model captures a slice of every hyperscaler’s custom silicon roadmap, including Nvidia’s own Vera CPU. That relationship makes our target look conservative if Arm’s per-chip take rate expands. Broadcom (NASDAQ:AVGO) is the sharper comp on custom AI silicon economics. It posted Q2 FY2026 AI semiconductor revenue of $10.8 billion, up 143% YoY, and guided Q3 AI revenue to $16 billion. Broadcom already runs a hyperscaler custom silicon business at scale, exactly where Arm is heading. Arm’s $271 price implies investors are willing to pay for the same trajectory earlier, making our 11% upside target measured rather than aggressive. The 24/7 Wall St. price target of $301.87 with 90% confidence backs a buy. The tipping factor is the AGI CPU demand book: $2 billion locked in across FY27-FY28 represents concrete, contracted demand. The setup looks constructive if Q1 FY27 lands inside guidance and data center royalty growth stays north of 50%. The thesis weakens if operating margins slip below 45% or the Qualcomm/Nuvia trial produces a materially adverse ruling. Extending the 24/7 Wall St. price target model forward and blending base and bull-case trajectories, here is where Arm could trade if the AGI CPU roadmap executes. These projections assume Arm executes on the $15 billion silicon business forecast and holds hyperscaler CPU share near 50%. Significant upside is possible if agentic AI CPU demand outpaces the 4x-per-gigawatt baseline, and downside if licensing disputes or export controls disrupt the royalty ramp. Title: Meta vs Pinterest: The Better Stock For Long-Term Investors
URL: https://247wallst.com/investing/2026/07/21/meta-vs-pinterest-the-better-stock-for-long-term-investors/
Time Published: 2026-07-21T16:30:37Z
Full Content:
Investing Meta and Pinterest both grew revenue double digits in Q1, but Meta's 40.6% operating margin towers over Pinterest's -3.3% GAAP loss. Analysts target $822 for META against a recent $644 price, with Polymarket assigning a 92.5% probability Meta beats Q2 earnings. Pinterest crossed $1 billion in quarterly revenue for the first time but drained cash to $378 million through aggressive buybacks. Act now: the analyst who called NVIDIA in 2010 just named his top 10 AI stocks — and Meta didn't make the cut. Grab the names FREE today. Sending You to Google News in 3 © 24/7 Wall St. Meta Platforms (NASDAQ:META | META Price Prediction) and Pinterest (NYSE:PINS) both closed Q1 2026 with double-digit revenue growth, yet the businesses behind those beats look nothing alike. Meta is pouring tens of billions into a superintelligence buildout that already reaches 3.56 billion daily users. Pinterest is a smaller visual discovery platform trying to turn 631 million monthly users into a durable ad engine. Meta beat Wall Street with EPS of $10.44 on $56.311 billion in revenue, up 33.08% year over year. Ad impressions climbed 19% while price per ad rose 12%, a rare combo that shows AI-driven targeting is still improving auction dynamics. A $8.03 billion tax benefit tied to U.S. Treasury Notice 2026-7 flattered the headline, so the underlying quarter looks strong but not quite as heroic as the headline suggests. CEO Mark Zuckerberg framed it as “a milestone quarter with strong momentum across our apps and the release of our first model from Meta Superintelligence Labs.” Reality Labs still bleeds cash at a $4.03 billion operating loss, and capex guidance jumped to $125 to $145 billion for the year. Pinterest posted $1.008 billion in revenue, up 17.84%, its first quarter above the billion-dollar mark. Rest of World revenue exploded 59% year over year, and Europe added 27%. Bill Ready keeps hammering the same theme: “Pinterest is where online discovery leads to real-world action.” The catch is a GAAP net loss of $73.6 million, driven by $231.45 million in stock-based comp and a $47.1 million restructuring charge. Meta is spending like a company chasing a decade-defining platform shift, and it can afford to. Return on equity sits at 32.9%. Pinterest carries a leaner sheet after loading up on $980 million in convertible notes and completing $1.946 billion in Class A buybacks, which drained cash from $969M to $378 million. That is an aggressive move for a company still running at a GAAP loss. For Meta, the question is whether ad monetization keeps outrunning capex. Polymarket traders assign a 92.5% probability Meta beats Q2, and analysts carry an average target of $822.69 against a recent price of $644.12. For Pinterest, I want to see ARPU accelerate beyond the current $1.61 and evidence the AI ads platform actually closes the monetization gap versus larger peers. If I have to pick one for a multi-year hold, I take Meta. The combination of 40.6% operating margins, a forward P/E near 21x, and a genuine seat at the superintelligence table is difficult to replicate. Pinterest fits a different investor: someone comfortable with a smaller, noisier turnaround story where shares have already recovered 11.27% since the May 4 earnings report. I would revisit that view if capex creeps above $145B without matching ad growth, or if Pinterest finally translates its 27% European and 59% Rest of World growth into positive GAAP earnings. Title: Nvidia says Vera Rubin is in full production, with OpenAI set to deploy at scale in Q3
URL: https://thenextweb.com/news/nvidia-vera-rubin-full-production-customers/
Time Published: 2026-07-21T16:03:10Z
Full Content:
Ian Buck told reporters at Nvidia headquarters that CoreWeave is already measuring a tenfold increase in token output from the new NVL72 racks, while the Vera CPU outpaces AMD on Python workloads Nvidia confirmed Vera Rubin is in full production, with OpenAI deploying at scale in Q3 and CoreWeave reporting ten times the token output. Nvidia confirmed on Monday that its Vera Rubin platform has reached full production, with Ian Buck, the company’s vice president of accelerated computing, telling reporters at Nvidia headquarters that systems are now shipping to customers including OpenAI, CoreWeave, Google Cloud, Microsoft Azure, Meta, and Dell. OpenAI plans to adopt Vera Rubin at scale during the third quarter, according to Bloomberg, which first reported the briefing. CoreWeave, one of the first cloud providers to receive the hardware, told Bloomberg that its NVL72 racks are delivering ten times the token output of the previous generation. The NVL72 is a full-rack system that pairs 72 Rubin GPUs with Vera CPUs and uses liquid cooling to eliminate internal cabling, a design change Nvidia demonstrated at the headquarters event. Buck said the cooling approach allows the company to remove copper connections that previously limited how tightly components could be packed together. The Vera CPU is the piece Nvidia built to replace the processors it previously bought from others, and the company used the briefing to draw direct comparisons with AMD. Nvidia claimed the Vera CPU is nearly twice as fast as AMD’s Turin chip on Python workloads, a benchmark chosen because Python dominates the software stack that runs most AI inference. Anthropic and OpenAI are among the first labs to receive the processor, alongside Perplexity, SpaceX, and Oracle. The 💜 of EU tech The latest rumblings from the EU tech scene, a story from our wise ol' founder Boris, and some questionable AI art. It's free, every week, in your inbox. Sign up now! The production milestone comes at an awkward moment for Nvidia’s stock. The company’s shares have risen nine percent this year, while the broader chip index has climbed 66 percent over the same period, according to Bloomberg. Intel, ARM, and AMD have all more than doubled. Analysts project Nvidia’s revenue will increase 82 percent to roughly $393 billion for the fiscal year, but that growth rate has not translated into the kind of share price momentum investors saw during the Blackwell cycle. Nvidia has steadily built out the Vera Rubin story over the past two months. Jensen Huang declared the platform in full production at Computex in early June and named Anthropic, OpenAI, SpaceX, and Oracle as early recipients. Monday’s briefing added the performance numbers and customer testimonials that the keynote stage did not provide, turning a claim into a set of measurable benchmarks. The numbers Nvidia chose to highlight are its own and its customers’ rather than independent tests, a distinction worth noting. CoreWeave’s tenfold token figure and the Python benchmark against AMD came from the company’s own briefing, not from a third party. Volume shipments across all named customers, and independent verification of the performance claims, are still ahead. With expertise in digital marketing, product management, and branding & identity, Ana Maria Constantin develops strategies that resonate (show all) With expertise in digital marketing, product management, and branding & identity, Ana Maria Constantin develops strategies that resonate with our target audience in the software/SaaS industry. Collaboration and teamwork are paramount to her, as she loves empowering her colleagues to achieve outstanding results and unlock their full potential.
